memo_markdown:
## Decision

**BOTTLENECK_NOT_EQUITY** — Western conversion and enrichment are genuine strategic constraints, but available evidence does not establish an underpriced, liquid listed equity with a complete per-share cash-flow bridge over the next 24 months.

Mode: `scan`. As-of and source cutoff: 2026-07-23. Horizon: 24 months. Benchmark: MSCI ACWI. Liquidity screen: approximately US$5 million average daily traded value. Research only; no leverage; no automatic trading.

## Funded demand

The payers are nuclear utilities and Western governments. Demand is more than aspirational:

- U.S. law has prohibited Russian LEU imports since 11 August 2024, subject to temporary waivers. Yet Russian-origin supply still represented 20% of SWU purchased by U.S. utilities in 2024, demonstrating a real replacement requirement rather than a purely political narrative.
- In January 2026, the U.S. Department of Energy awarded US$2.7 billion of milestone-based enrichment task orders over ten years.
- Existing-reactor refuelling is the dependable unit driver; advanced-reactor HALEU demand is less certain and more schedule-sensitive.

Demand can fail to tighten the market within 24 months if waivers remain heavily used, inventories bridge the transition, reactor projects slip, or incumbent additions arrive on schedule.

## System map

The mandatory chain is:

`uranium concentrate → conversion to UF6 → enrichment measured in SWU → fuel fabrication and qualification → reactor reload`

Conversion and enrichment are technically mandatory for ordinary light-water-reactor fuel. The constraint nodes are qualified UF6 conversion output and non-Russian centrifuge capacity. Relevant roles are:

- `owner`: operator of qualified conversion or enrichment assets;
- `unlocker`: equipment or engineering provider enabling new cascades;
- `substitute`: alternative enrichment technology or non-Russian supply route;
- `tollbooth`: mandatory licensing, qualification or fuel-service interface;
- `absorber`: incumbent with qualified spare or ramping capacity;
- `public_proxy`: listed entity with indirect or minority exposure.

Qualification, nuclear licensing, specialised centrifuge technology, feed inventories and long construction lead times constrain rapid substitution. However, waiver use, inventories, underfeeding and funded incumbent expansion are credible workarounds.

## Constraint proof

Constraint score: **84.0/100**.

Urenco reported 17.2 million SWU/year of capacity at the end of 2025 and 2.5 million SWU of committed additions. Orano reported 7.5 million SWU/year at Georges Besse II and a €1.7 billion expansion. These figures simultaneously prove concentration and undermine a perpetual-shortage thesis.

Estimated scarcity clock: P10 12 months, P50 30 months, P90 60 months. The inference that tightness lasts through much of the investment horizon has medium confidence; an acute physical shortage remains unproved because U.S. utility inventories were 126 million pounds U3O8e at the end of 2024 and waivers remain available.

## Security map

- **Cameco — NYSE:CCJ / TSX:CCO (`owner`)**: owns Port Hope conversion capacity and has operating exposure, but conversion economics are blended with refining and fuel manufacturing.
- **Centrus Energy — NYSE American:LEU (`public_proxy`/prospective owner)**: direct strategic enrichment exposure, but roughly US$2.3 billion of its end-2025 LEU backlog depended on constructing new capacity and securing substantial public and private investment.
- **Silex Systems — ASX:SLX (`substitute`)**: majority exposure to pre-commercial laser enrichment through GLE; qualification, funding and scale-up remain binary.
- **Urenco, Orano and ConverDyn (`owner`/`absorber`)**: much of the most direct operating exposure is privately held or state-controlled and therefore unavailable as globally listed equity.
- No liquid, material pure-play `unlocker` or `tollbooth` passed verification.

The verified ownership chain for the selected listed proxy is `Port Hope facility → Cameco Corporation → NYSE:CCJ / TSX:CCO`.

## Equity capture

Cameco’s 2025 Fuel Services segment reported CAD562 million revenue and CAD219 million adjusted EBITDA, produced 11.2 million kgU of UF6, delivered 13.1 million kgU of fuel-services products and held about 83 million kgU of contracted UF6 conversion volume.

That is not a complete shareholder bridge. Public disclosure does not isolate conversion revenue, conversion margins, attributable capex and working capital, interest, tax, fully diluted forecast shares, or contract-reset and cancellation economics. Consequently:

`system scarcity → conversion revenue → FCF → fully diluted per-share FCF`

cannot be reproduced. `equity_bridge.complete=false` and `no_material_revenue_bridge=true`. Strong physical evidence cannot compensate for this hard failure.

## Three clocks

- Physical scarcity: P10/P50/P90 = **12/30/60 months**.
- Candidate monetisation lag: **6 months**, reflecting existing deliveries and contracts.
- Market discovery: **3 months**; the theme is already widely recognised.
- Modelled monetisable runway: **24 months**.

The physical clock is favourable, but the discovery clock is largely closed and the shareholder-capture clock cannot be quantified at conversion-only per-share level.

## Valuation

The expectations/mispricing score is only **36.5/100**, below the 45 gate. Current price and same-day liquidity were not independently archived, so the valuation conclusion is deliberately low-confidence rather than precision-priced.

Research scenarios for the selected listed proxy relative to its as-of price:

- Bear, 35% probability: **−35%** — inventories and additions normalise fuel-cycle pricing and the diversified nuclear premium compresses.
- Base, 45%: **+8%** — contracts support earnings, but the bottleneck is recognised and diluted by other businesses.
- Bull, 20%: **+40%** — contracting accelerates while incumbent expansions slip.

Probability-weighted return is **−0.65%**. These are assumptions, not a price target or trading instruction. Model output: core geometric score 67.549; final score 54.208; hard gates failed.

## Catalysts

- 2026–2027: reported progress and commissioning of Urenco and Orano additions.
- Each Cameco reporting period: Fuel Services deliveries, realised pricing, EBITDA margin and contract additions.
- 2026–2027: Centrus construction funding, capital deployment and conversion of contingent backlog.
- September 2027 or next EIA release: utility inventories, Russian-origin SWU and forward contracting.
- DOE milestone certifications and appropriations under the January 2026 awards.

## Red team

The strongest alternative causal explanation is **policy-funded capital-cycle normalisation**, not persistent scarcity. Waivers and inventories can bridge utilities until Urenco, Orano and subsidised U.S. capacity arrive. Under this explanation, high contract prices are the incentive that cures the shortage; economic rents accrue mainly to private/state-controlled incumbents, engineering supply chains, customers holding inventory, or governments rather than minority shareholders in listed proxies.

The documented negative search covered waivers, utility inventories, new capacity, underfeeding, customer dual sourcing, contingent backlog, financing and dilution, technology qualification, contract escape terms and demand slippage.

## Kill switches

- Urenco plus Orano additions entering service exceed 2.0 million SWU cumulatively by 2027-12-31.
- Fuel Services adjusted EBITDA margin falls below 25% for two consecutive half-years while annualised deliveries remain at least 12 million kgU.
- Russian waiver use remains near statutory limits and U.S. utility inventories remain above 100 million pounds U3O8e at year-end 2026.

Conversely, the normalisation alternative would be falsified by materially lower waiver volumes, failed Western capacity ramps, accelerating long-term prices and contracting despite inventory drawdown.

## Portfolio fit

Portfolio decision: **no position; research watchlist only**. The machine-readable portfolio contains zero weights and therefore implies no order.

Causal overlap is high: CCJ–LEU share Western fuel-security policy, Russian displacement and utility contracting; LEU–SLX additionally share U.S. subsidy and advanced-reactor demand. CCJ–SLX overlap is medium-high. A three-ticker basket would therefore be one concentrated policy/fuel-cycle exposure, not three independent ideas. MSCI ACWI is the broad global-equity comparison benchmark; it covered about 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set and 2,461 constituents at 30 June 2026.

## Open questions

1. What portion of Fuel Services revenue, EBITDA, capex and working capital is attributable solely to UF6 conversion?
2. What are the price-reset, floor, ceiling and cancellation mechanics of the 83 million kgU conversion book?
3. What is the 2026–2028 fully diluted share bridge after SBC, converts, warrants and other contingent claims?
4. How much Russian LEU enters under waivers in 2026–2027, and how quickly do utility inventories decline?
5. Which announced enrichment additions achieve qualified commercial output by July 2028?
6. Are current listed-security valuations already discounting the bull case? Same-day price, EV and ADV evidence was not archived.

Until these questions are resolved, physical scarcity should not be translated into a listed-equity recommendation.

## Sources

- [DOE Russian uranium ban waiver guidance](https://www.energy.gov/ne/russian-uranium-ban-waiver-guidance)
- [EIA Uranium Marketing Annual Report](https://www.eia.gov/uranium/marketing/)
- [DOE US$2.7 billion enrichment awards, 5 January 2026](https://www.energy.gov/articles/us-department-energy-awards-27-billion-restore-american-uranium-enrichment)
- [Urenco 2025 annual report](https://www.urenco.com/cdn/uploads/supporting-files/Urenco_2025_annual_report.pdf)
- [Orano 2025 annual report—conversion and enrichment](https://www.orano.group/finance-raa/2025/en/39/)
- [Cameco 2025 annual report](https://www.cameco.com/invest/financial-information/annual-reports/2025)
- [Centrus 2025 annual report](https://investors.centrusenergy.com/static-files/8375eda8-a5d9-4f3f-bba8-5bc2d3c072a1)
- [Centrus 2025 results and 2026 guidance](https://investors.centrusenergy.com/news-releases/news-release-details/centrus-reports-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-2025-results-and)
- [MSCI ACWI index profile](https://www.msci.com/indexes/index/892400/msci-acwi-index)
- [World Nuclear Association 2025 fuel report](https://world-nuclear.org/our-association/publications/global-trends-reports/world-nuclear-fuel-report-2025)

Secondary search results were used only for lead generation, not critical confirmation.

Canonical validation attempt 1 used exactly:
- `python scripts/validate_evidence.py - < evidence.json` — exit 127; stdout empty; stderr `zsh: command not found: python`.
- `python scripts/score_opportunity.py - --format json < opportunity.json` — exit 127; stdout empty; same stderr.
- `python scripts/analyze_portfolio_clusters.py - < portfolio.json` — exit 127; stdout empty; same stderr.
No artifact defect was indicated; the environment lacked a `python` command.
